slides.heading: Meet Arjun — a digital artist
slides.body:
Arjun is designing a logo for a car company. He's been working on it for hours and the design is looking really good.

Then his client asks: "Can we try a bold red version? But DON'T lose the blue one — we might want to go back."

What does Arjun do?
bullets: 🎨 He has one canvas. One file.
😰 If he paints over it, the blue version is GONE
🤔 What would you do in this situation?

slides.heading: What artists do in real life
slides.body:
Real artists — painters, sculptors, photographers — have solved this problem for centuries.

A painter takes a PHOTO of their canvas at each important stage. A sculptor makes a cast before carving further.

They create checkpoints. Moments they can always return to.

Tap each checkpoint below to see what Arjun saved at each stage.
bullets: ↩️ Can return to ANY of these, any time

slides.heading: The insight: save checkpoints, not copies
slides.body:
Notice what Arjun does NOT do:

  ❌ logo_final.psd
  ❌ logo_final_blue.psd
  ❌ logo_ACTUALLY_final_USE_THIS.psd

Instead, he has ONE project file — and a TIMELINE of checkpoints.

Every checkpoint has a label: "what changed and why."

He can jump to any moment in history. This is the idea behind Git.
bullets: ✅ Every checkpoint labelled with a message
✅ Jump back to any moment, instantly
✅ Try bold experiments — the safe version is always saved

slides.heading: Code has the same problem
slides.body:
Code files are like Arjun's canvas.

You write your app. It works. You try something new. It breaks. You want to go back to when it worked — but you saved over everything.

This is the moment every developer realizes they needed Git.

In the next step, meet Priya — a developer going through exactly this pain.
bullets: 💻 Code is just a file, like a canvas
🐛 New features can break working code
😱 Without checkpoints, there's no way back
→ Next: See the real problems Priya faces
